‘The current flows between these two tips and hence it does not need a return electrode, unlike the
unipolar cautery.
Option C: The harmonic scalpel (shown below) is a surgical instrument used to simultaneously cut
and cauterize tissue. Ultrasonic energy is used in Harmonic scalpel where ultrasonic energy is
converted to mechanical energy at the active blade
Option D: LigaSure vessel sealing system (shown below) is a bipolar apparatus for sealing vascular
tissue. It applies a high coaptive pressure during the generation of tissue temperature under
1000°C, hydrogen cross-links are first ruptured and then renatured, resulting in a vascular seal
that has high tensile strength.

subject: Surgery
question: Radical neck dissection spares which of the following structures?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 25:
Digastric muscle is spared in radical neck dissection (RND). RND is used in the treatment of
malignant lesions of the neck with local spread or metastasis.
In 1905, Crile described RND where Level I-V LN, sternocleidomastoid (SCM), internal jugular
vein (IJV), and XI cranial nerve (spinal accessory nerve) are removed.
In 1962, Suarez described modified RND (MRND) where Level I-V LNs are removed and the
following are preserved:
+ MRND I - XI cranial nerve

question: A patient comes with active variceal bleeding. Which of the following is not done?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 26:
Platelet transfusion is not done in the case of active variceal bleeding unless coagulopathy is
confirmed as the cause. Platelet transfusions are indicated only in patients with a platelet count of
<50,000/microL or in patients with evidence of microvascular bleeding,
Variceal hemorrhage is a medical emergency. Varices usually present with the acute
onset large-volume haematemesis, the lower esophagus being the most common site for variceal
bleeding,
The sites for portosystemic anastomosis are:
+ Left gastric vein to distal oesophageal veins: oesophageal and gastric variceal bleeding

lumbar, intercostal, and phrenic veins
+ Omental and colonic veins near hepatic and splenic exures to Retroperitoneal veins near
hepatic and splenic mexures
Medical management of variceal bleeding includes blood transfusion (option B) and drug therapy
with somatostatin analogs like octreotide (option C). Short-term antibiotics like ceftriaxone are
known to decrease bacterial infection and increase survival.
Surgical measures are preferred if medical management fails. They include balloon tamponade,
endoscopic banding (option D), transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS),
portosystemic shunts, splenectomy, and gastric revascularization
Note: Sugiura procedure is the non-shunt surgical management of refractory oesophageal variceal
bleeding. It is a procedure of de-vascularizing the distal esophagus and stomach.

subject: Surgery
question: A child presents with pain and swelling in the right side of his scrotum and testicular torsion is suspected. Which is the most appropriate management?
options: Immediate exploration on both symptomatic and asymptomatic side
Immediate exploration on symptomatic side only
Delayed exploration on both symptomatic and asymptomatic side
Delayed exploration on symptomatic side only

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 27:
Ina case of suspected testicular torsion, immediate exploration needs to be done on both the
symptomatic as well as the asymptomatic side. After the de-torsion, the adequacy of the blood
flow has to be assessed by Doppler.
In testicular torsion, the blood supply to the testicle is compromised due to the twisting of the
spermatic cord within the tunica vaginalis, resulting in ischemia of the epididymis and the testis.
The patient usually presents with acute onset pain and swelling of the scrotum. On examination,
the cremasteric reflex is lost, and the scrotum looks asymmetrical.
Prehn’s sign is used to differentiate epididymitis from testicular torsion. Prehn's sign is said to be
positive when the pain gets relieved on lifting the testis, and it is seen in epididymo-orchitis.
4724
More than 80% of the testis can be salvaged if surgery is performed within 6 hours. If the testis is
found to be necrosed on scrotal exploration, orchidectomy of the necrosed testis and orchidopexy
of the contralateral testis is done.

subject: Surgery
question: Which of the following is the principle behind the wound-healing procedure shown in the image below?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 29:
The given image shows negative pressure wound therapy using a vacuum-assisted closure (VAC)
device to promote healing in chronic wounds.
Principle: An intermittent negative pressure of -125 mmHg hastens debridement and formation of
granulation tissue in chronic wounds.
4726
Contraindications:
+ Exposed vital structures (organs, blood vessels, or vascular grafts)
+ Preexisting infection
+ Inadequate debridement of devitalized soft tissue or bone increases the risk of infection.
+ The presence of malignancy (Marjolin’s ulcer)
+ An allergic reaction towards the adhesive used to maintain the vacuum seal.
‘Though not an absolute contraindication, there is no appreciable benefit for ischemic wounds.

subject: Surgery
question: Stage the bedsore in the following image:

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 30:
The given image shows complete skin loss that involves muscles and shows exposed bone
indicating a stage 4 pressure sore.
Pressure sores are skin or soft tissue injuries that occur due to prolonged pressure over specific
areas of the body. An external pressure of >30 mmHg leads to the formation of pressure sores.
The common sites of pressure sores in decreasing frequency are the ischium, greater trochanter,
sacrum, heel, malleolus (lateral followed by medial), and occiput.
Pressure sores can be prevented with good skincare, the use of air-fluidized beds, special
pressure-dispersion cushions, or foams. Repositioning the patient every two hours can lessen the
pressure on the area.
Ulcers of stages 1 and 2 often do not require operative measures. They are managed with regular
wound cleaning and dressing with a good antibiotic cover:
Stage 3 and 4 uleers are adequately debrided, and vacuum-assisted closure (VAC) is used to
promote the growth of granulation tissue required for surgical closure. Large skin flaps that have
muscle with sensory innervation are best for reconstruction.

subject: Surgery
question: A patient with a road traffic accident presented to the emergency room and requires a large amount of fluids to be transfused. Which of the following IV cannula is preferred?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 31:
Grey coloured IV cannula is required for this patient in order to transfuse large amount of fluids.
The ATLS 10th edition guidelines state that 2 cannulae of atleast 18G should be used to gain
vascular access in trauma patients. In this scenario requiring large fluid transfusions, the grey
(16G) cannula would be preferable to the green (18G) cannula due to the higher flow rate.

+ Closed : They carry out their actions through suetion
Jackson-pratt drain is a closed, air-tight drainage system that operates by self-suction. The drain
is sutured in place on the skin at the site of insertion to promote stability. There are two main

subject: Surgery
question: A 65-year-old man presents with abdominal pain for the past 6 hours, BP- 89/5ommHg, RR- 24/min, saturation 92% with 6 L of oxygen. Radiograph shows extraluminal air in the abdomen. Which of the following must be done before shifting this patient into the OT?
options: Insert central venous catheter as initial IV access of choice

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 1:
Volume resuscitation with 1 liter of crystalloid infusion should be done before shifting this patient
to the OT for an emergency exploratory laparotomy.
The given clinical scenario in a patient with hypotension and tachypnea with extraluminal gas in
the abdomen is suggestive of perforation peritonitis with hemodynamic instability. The immediate
step is volume resuscitation to maintain circulating volume and organ perfusion.
Peritonitis is the inflammation of the peritoneum and can be categorized as local or diffuse and
acute or chronic,
‘Causes:
+ Gastrointestinal perforation e.g. perforated ulcer, appendix, diverticulum
+ Transmural translocation (no perforation), e.
peritonitis
pancreatitis, ischaemic bowel, primary bacterial
+ Exogenous contamination, e.g. drains, open surgery, trauma, peritoneal dialysis
+ Female genital tract infection, e.g. pelvic inflammatory disease
+ Haematogenous spread (rare), e.g. septicaemia
+ Miscellaneous- e.g. familial Mediterranean fever
Clinical features:
+ Abdominal pain, worse on movement, coughing and deep respiration
+ Constitutional symptoms: anorexia, malaise, fever, lassitude
4746
+ Tachycardia
+ Tenderness +/- guarding/rigidity/rebound of the abdominal wall
+ Pain/tenderness on rectal/vaginal examination (pelvic peritonitis)
+ Absent or reduced bowel sounds
+ Septic shock (systemic inflammatory response syndrome [SIRS] and multiorgan dysfunction
syndrome [MODS)) in later stages
Management:
General care of the patient:
+ Correction of fluid and electrolyte imbalance
+ Insertion of a nasogastric tube and urinary catheter
+ Broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy
+ Aprropriate analgesia
Surgical treatment: Exploratory laparotomy and repair of perforation is the definitive
‘management. Presence of peritoneal signs and hemodynamic instability are indications for urgent
surgery.
Other options
Option A: Intubation is not indicated by the patient's current vital signs.
Option B: Initial volume resuscitation can be done with the peripheral large-bore cannula. A
central venous catheter may be needed if peripheral access is dificult, vasopressors are
administered, patient requires TPN.
Option D: A CT scan, while helpful for locating lesion and confirming the diagnosis, is not
indicated for a hemodynamically unstable patient.
Note:
According to recent ATLS guidelines, crystalloid infusion should be limited to a maximum of 1.5
litres as crystalloid resuscitation beyond 1.5 liters inereases the risk of death.

question: A patient with recurrent pain in the right hypochondrium, nausea, and vomiting is diagnosed with biliary dyskinesia. Which of the following is least useful in the diagnosis of this condition?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 2:
USG is the least useful investigation in the diagnosis of biliary dyskinesia or sphincter of Oddi
dysfunction since the findings can be normal or show non-specific changes such as dilatation of
the common bile duct.
Sphincter of Oddi dysfunction (SOD) is caused due to a structurally or physiologically abnormal
sphincter with a higher tone, which fails to relax, causing pain and recurrent episodes of
pancreatitis with normal liver function tests. There are two clinical types: biliary SOD and
pancreatic SOD. Each type is further classified into types 1,2 and 3.

&t;35% is considered diagnostic of biliary dyskinesia.

syndrome; hence, post-surgery follow-up is essential.

subject: Surgery
question: A 32-year-old woman was declared brain dead. Her family has decided to donate her organs. Who is the ideal/best recipient?
options: 14 year old child with multi organ dysfunction syndrome
50 year alcoholic man with liver cirrhosis with 1 month abstinence
70 year old woman with end stage renal disease
32 year old woman with renal failure

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 3:
Among the given options, a 32-year-old woman with renal failure is the most suitable recipient for
transplantation,
This is because the prognosis for the other patients mentioned above is poor. Prioritization of
organ transplant recepients should be done based on post-transplant outcomes.
Option A - 14-year-old with multi-organ dysfunction syndrome is not suitable because there is a
poor prognosis for the ongoing organ dysfunction.
Option B - Alcoholic patients with just 1 month of abstinence are not considered for a transplant.
Option C - A 70-year-old woman with end-stage renal disease is not the right candidate because
the chances of long-term survival and a successful outcome are very less.

subject: Surgery
question: Question text was not included in the source. Pick the correct option below.
options: I d) Acute pancreatitis without choledocholithiaisis

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 4:
ERCP is least useful in the diagnosis of acute pancreatitis without choledocholithiasis.
ERCP stands for Endoscopic Retrograde Cholangio-Pancreatography. It involves the use of a
side-viewing endoscope which is passed through the pylorus of the stomach into the second part
of the duodenum to visualise the papilla. This is then cannulated, and dye is injected into the
pancreatic or bile duct to visualise the pancreatic or biliary tree. It can be therapeutic or
diagnostic. It is the best diagnostic modality for viewing the distal biliary tree, but MRCP is
preferred as the diagnostic modality unless a therapeutic intervention is also needed.
Indications of ERCP:
+ Choledocholithiasis,
+ Acute cholangitis,
+ Biliary stricture, biliary leak
+ Pancreatic duct stricture, pancreatic stones
+ Extrahepatic biliary strictures related to primary sclerosing cholangitis
+ Endoscopic therapy for some patients with sphincter of Oddi dysfunction
4748
+ Sphineterotomy
The most common therapeutic indication for ERCP is the relief of biliary obstruction due to
gallstone disease and benign or malignant biliary strictures.
Contraindications of ERCP include patients who cannot tolerate anesthesia, patients with type 3
sphincter of Oddi dysfunction, luminal gastrointestinal obstruction (examination limited to an
area proximal to the level of obstruction and untreated hemostatic disorder (high risk of
bleeding).
Complications of ERCP include duodenal perforation, hemorrhage, pancreatitis and sepsis.
Other options:
Option A: ERCP can help in ruling out the etiology in cases of recurrent pancreatitis.
Option B: ERCP can act as both diagnostic and therapeutic in cases of unexplained jaundice.
Option C: ERCP can be useful in ease of a periampullary mass to take a tissue biopsy and cytology.

question: Which of the following nerve is least injured during thyroid surgery?

question: eFAST is least helpful in diagnosing which of the following conditions?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 7:
Among the given options, eFAST (focussed assessment with sonography in trauma) is the least
useful in diagnosing retroperitoneal hematoma as the sensitivity of ultrasound for the detection of
retroperitoneal hematomas is extremely poor.
Computed Tomography (CT Scan) is the investigation of choice for retroperitoneal injury, but it
can only be done in a hemodynamically stable patient.
FAST stands for Focussed Assessment with Sonography for Trauma. It is a rapid, non-invasive
bedside test for detecting free fluid using sonography used to assess patients for potential
thoracoabdominal injuries
Technique:
+ The patient is placed in a supine position and the following regions are scanned:
+ Subxiphoid transverse view: assess for pericardial effusion and left lobe liver injuries
+ Longitudinal view of the right upper quadrant: assess for right liver injuries, right kidney injury,
and Morison pouch
+ Longitudinal view of the left upper quadrant: assess for splenic injury and left kidney injury
+ Transverse and longitudinal views of the suprapubic region: assess the bladder and pouch of
Douglas
eFAST stands for extended FAST and incorporates left and right thoracic views to assess for
pneumothorax and haemothorax.
Limitations of eFAST are:
+ Operator-dependent
+ Bowel gas and subcutaneous air distort images.
+ Can miss diaphragm, bowel, and pancreatic injuries
+ Unreliable for assessment of the retroperitoneum

question: A patient with unilateral breast cancer is started on tamoxifen. This will increase the risk of?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 10:
‘Tamoxifen increases the risk of endometrial cancer by 1-2%.
‘The benefit of tamoxifen in breast cancer outweighs the risk of endometrial cancer.
Tamoxifen is a selective estrogen receptor modulator, approved for the treatment of estrogen
receptor-positive (ER) breast cancer. It is used only in premenopausal women for 5 years in
low-risk patients and for 10 years in patients with a high risk of relapse (node-positive, tumor
&gt:5 cm, lymphovascular invasion). It is also known to reduce the incidence of second primary
breast cancer in the contralateral breast.
‘Tamoxifen increases the risk of endometrial cancer in postmenopausal women; hence aromatase
inhibitors are used in postmenopausal women. Tamoxifen is also not recommended in patients
who have had bilateral mastectomies with ER-positive ductal carcinoma in situ. Other side effects
of tamoxifen include bone pain, hot flashes, nausea, vomiting, fluid retention and rarely

subject: Surgery
question: Which of the following is the most appropriate mesh for use in hernioplasty?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 11:
The most appropriate mesh used in hernioplasty is a lightweight mesh with large pores.
Lightweight meshes generally contain less material and induce a less-pronounced foreign body
reaction and decreased inflammatory response, which results in better tissue incorporation,
increased prosthesis compliance, and decreased patient discomfort and pain.
Meshes with large pores exhibit less inflammatory infiltrate, connective tissue, and scar bridging,
which allows increased soft tissue ingrowth. They have greater elasticity and flexibility and also
shrink less resulting in lesser adhesions, better tissue incorporation, and decreased patient
discomfort and pain. So the best mesh type among the given options is lightweight mesh with
large pores.
Meshes used in hernioplasty can be synthetic or biological depending on their composition,
Synthetic meshes are made from polypropylene, polyester, or PTFE (polytetrafluoroethylene).
PTFE meshes are hydrophobic with a lower risk of adhesion.
Biological meshes are acellular matrix materials derived from humans or animals and have lesser
complications. But due to poor collagen deposition and absorption, they rapidly lose mechanical
strength and hence their use is restricted to infected environments.
Note: To promote better tissue integration, a composite mesh was developed which combines the
attributes of polypropylene and PTFE by layering the two substances on top of one another.

subject: Surgery
question: Which of the following is not an adverse effect seen after a massive blood transfusion?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 13:
Hypercalcemia is not an adverse effect of massive blood transfusion.
Massive blood transfusion instead leads to hypocalcemia as the anticoagulant citrate binds to
calcium. The lethal triad of massive blood transfusion is acidosis, hypothermia, and coagulopathy.

subject: Surgery
question: Which of the following gene mutations are involved in carcinoma of the colon?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 14:
APC, k-ras, and p53 gene mutations are implicated in carcinoma of the colon.
In the adenoma to carcinoma sequence, the loss of one normal copy of the tumor suppressor gene
APCs the first hit, followed by the loss of the intact second copy of the APC gene (second hit)
Other changes in the sequence include the mutation of k-ras and the inactivation of the tumor
suppressor gene p53, leading to the emergence of carcinoma. The accumulation of these
mutations is more important than their occurrence in a specifie order. This is known as the
Fearon-Vogelstein adenoma-carcinoma multistep model of colorectal neoplasia.
4755

subject: Surgery
question: A 25-year-old male patient was brought to the ER after RTA, his RR is 25/min, BP is 80/45mmHg. On examination, hyper-resonance is heard over the left side of the chest. The chest X-ray performed is given below. What would be the appropriate management in this case?
options: Wide bore needle insertion in the 5th intercostal space (ICS)
Wide bore needle insertion in the 5th ICS followed by an intercostal drain in the 5th ICS

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 17:
The given clinical scenario of hemodynamic instability, hyper-resonance on the left side of the
chest, and the X-ray findings are suggestive of tension pneumothorax. It is managed by inserting a
wide-bore needle in the 5th intercostal space (ICS) followed by placing an intercostal drain in the
5th ICs.
‘Traumatic pneumothorax can be:
+ Simple pneumothorax: Without hemodynamic instability
+ Tension pneumothorax: With hemodynamic instability
‘The X-ray given below is showing a left-sided tension pneumothorax. A large, well-demarcated
area is devoid of lung markings on the left side. The yellow arrows are pointing to the tracheal
deviation and the mediastinal shift to the right side.
Management:
+ Simple pneumothorax: Tube thoracostomy or chest tube insertion in the sth ICS in the triangle
of safety
+ Tension pneumotho
+ Immediate management: Urgent needle decompression or needle thoracostomy i. insertion of
a wide-bore needle in the 5th ICS, anterior to the midaxillary line
+ Definitive management: Tube thoracostomy or chest tube insertion in the 5th ICS in the triangle
of safety
Note: According to ATLS 1oth edition
+ In adults, needle thoracostomy or needle decompression location is changed to 5th ICS anterior
to the midaxillary line from 2nd ICS midelavicular line.
+ In the pediatric age group, the needle thoracostomy location remains at the 2nd ICS
midclavicular line.

question: A patient presents with pain in the right upper quadrant on the second postoperative day following a cholecystectomy. Evaluation reveals a collection in the subhepatic space. A bile leak was suspected. Which of the following is the best investigation for this patient?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 18:
Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) is the best investigation in a patient
suspected of post-cholecystectomy biliary leaks.
Patients with post-cholecystectomy biliary leak usually present with fever, jaundice and
abdominal pain. USG is used for an initial evaluation in such cases and can show a collection in
the sub-hepatie space.
‘The most common bile leak following cholecystectomy is from the cystic duct. This can be treated
by placing a biliary endoprosthesis (stent) in the common bile duct across the origin of the cystic
duct.
4759
ERCP is the best investigation as it has the advantage of being both diagnostic and
therapeutic. ERCP is useful post-cholecystectomy as there is a high likelihood of a retained stone
and stone extraction and/or temporary stenting may be required.
HIDA scan is the most sensitive investigation for biliary leakage.
CT scan can show focal fluid collections, ascites, biliary obstruction, hepatic atrophy, signs of
secondary biliary cirrhosis and any vascular injury.

subject: Surgery
question: A 35-year-old chronic smoker presents with bluish discoloration of the toe along with intermittent claudication. Which of the following will be the next appropriate investigation in this patient?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 21:
Chronic smoker presenting with bluish discoloration of the toe along with intermittent
claudication is suggestive of peripheral arterial disease and duplex scanning will be the next
appropriate investigation.
Duplex Scanning is a major non-invasive technique that uses B-mode ultrasound to provide an
image of the vessel. Most scanners now have color coding, which allows detailed visualization of
blood flow in a vessel. Different colors indicate changes in the direction and velocity of flow, with
areas of high flow usually indicating stenotic lesions.
Other options:
Option A: CT Angiography (CTA) is more useful in the evaluation of visceral arterial disease (eg.
mesenteric ischemia). It can be beneficial in peripheral artery diseases where a duplex scan shows
poor images (aortoiliac segment involvement). CTA is not routinely recommended in peripheral
artery disease because of concomitant renal dysfunction in these patients which can lead to
contrast-induced nephropathy.
Option B: MR Angiography is time-consuming and expensive, hence not done as the next
investigation. It is contraindicated in patients with claustrophobia and metallic implants.
Option C: Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) is an invasive and expensive
procedure compared to other investigations. The benefits of DSA are that it provides dynamic
arterial flow information and can be combined with a definitive endovascular intervention when
indicated. DSA is indicated only when there is a discrepancy in diagnosis or intervention is
planned. Complications associated with DSA include bleeding, hematoma, false aneurysm
formation, thrombosis, arterial dissection, distal embolisation, renal dysfunction, and allergie
reaction.

subject: Surgery
question: A 40-year-old woman presents with complaints of swelling of the right breast over the past 15 days. On examination, there was local rise of temperature, and the swelling was mobile over the chest wall. What is the likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 22:
In the given scenario, the rapid onset of the disease and the examination findings are suggestive
of inflammatory carcinoma of the breast.
Inflammatory breast cancer is the most aggressive subtype of breast cancer. It mimies acute
mastitis. The hallmark of inflammatory breast carcinoma is the diffuse tumor involvement of the
dermal lymphatics within the breast and the overlying skin, often without a discrete underlying
tumor mass (peau d' orange appearance). The patient presents with rapidly progressive swelling of
the breast, warmth, erythema and edema. A breast lump may or may not be present.
A diagnosis of inflammatory breast carcinoma should be suspected when a patient with rapidly
progressive swelling with inflammatory skin changes does not respond to antibiotics. The imaging
features with ultrasound and a mammograph is non-specific due to the underlying erythema and
edema. A biopsy should be done to confirm the diagnosis.
‘Treatment includes combined-modality treatment:
+ Neoadjuvant chemotherapy
+ Mastectomy
+ Radiation therapy
+ Hormonal therapy- for EI
-positive tumors
+ Trastuzumab for HER-2-positive tumors
Option B: Giant fibroadenomas occasionally occur during puberty. These are benign solid
encapsulated slow-growing tumors over 5 cm in diameter. It presents as a painless lump in the
breast. They are managed by excision via a submammary incision.
Option C: Phyllodes tumors usually occur in women over the age of 40 years but can appear in
younger women. Phyllodes tumor is mostly benign even though it is also called cystosarcoma
phyllodes. It presents as a large tumor with an unevenly bosselated surface. They are usually
joc exeision, massive tumours, recurrent wits ana mangnant sions are weatea wiun a
mastectomy.
Option D: Galactocele are the most common benign breast lesions in lactating women. It is a
cystic enlargement of the mammary gland containing milk. They are mostly detected during
lactation or in the third trimester of pregnancy but rarely, may also present after stopping
breastfeeding. Clinically, the mass is usually firm and non-tender and presents as a solid
tumour-like lesion on physical examination. The patient usually notes the
lesion during lactation or some period after lactation. Needle aspiration, followed
by excision should it recur, is the standard method of treating galactocele.

subject: Surgery
question: A middle-aged man presents with progressive difficulty in swallowing for both solids and liquids for 8 months. Barium swallow done is shown below. What other investigations need to be done in this patient?
options: Upper GI endoscopy and 24 hour PH monitoring

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 23:
The given clinical scenario and the barium swallow showing a bird beak appearance are suggestive
of achalasia cardia.
Achalasia is a primary esophageal motility disorder characterized by the absence
of esophageal peristalsis and impaired relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) in
response to swallowing,
Manometry is considered the most sensitive investigation to diagnose achalasia as it identifies
early disease before esophageal dilatation and food retention. The diagnostic criteria for achalasia
with manometry are impaired lower esophageal sphincter (LES) relaxation and absent
peristalsis with an elevated LES pressure.
Endoscopy is useful for ruling out pseudo-achalasia.
Note: 24-hour pH monitoring is ideal and most accurate in patients with the features of
gastroesophageal reflux disease.

subject: Surgery
question: A 55-year-old man with IHD presents with sudden, severe, generalized abdominal pain and multiple episodes of vomiting. On examination, the pulse rate is 118bpm, rhythm is irregularly irregular, BP is 90/60 mmHg. The abdomen appears soft, non-distended, and bowel sounds are absent. Which of the following could be a likely diagnosis in this case?

explanation.detail:
Solt
n to Question 24:
The given clinical scenario of a patient with sudden onset severe abdominal pain which is
out-of- proportion to the physical examination findings along with atrial fibrillation is suggestive of
acute mesenteric ischemia.
Mesenteric ischemia may be acute or chronic. The superior mesenteric artery (SMA) is most likely
affected. An embolus is the most common cause of SMA occlusion. Possible sources for SMA
emboisation include:
+ Left atrium in atrial fibrillation
+ Previous history of ischemic heart disease/myocardial infarction
+ Vegetations on mitral and aortic valves associated with endocarditis
+ An atheromatous plaque from an aortic aneurysm.
Other causes of mesenteric ischemia include:
4765
+ Venous thrombosis in factor V Leiden mutation
+ Non-occlusive mesenteric ischemia due to alterations in splanchnic blood flow.
‘The most important clue to an early diagnosis of acute mesenteric ischemia is the sudden onset
of severe abdominal pain in a patient with atrial fibrillation or atherosclerosis. The pain is
typically in the central abdomen and out of proportion to the physical findings.
Associated clinical features include nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal distension, peritonitis,
and the passage of bloody stools.
The gold standard for the diagnosis of acute mesenteric ischemia is angiography, and
management is laparotomy. The goal of operative exploration is to resect the compromised
bowel and restore blood supply via embolectomy.
Other options:
Option B: A pulsatile abdominal mass is seen in the case of an abdominal aortic aneurysm. Acute
pain and hypotension occur with rupture of the aneurysm, which requires an emergency
operation,
Options A and D: Acute severe pancreatitis and peptic uleer perforation have no association with
atrial fibrillation. They usually present with pain over the upper abdomen associated with
guarding and rigidity on examination.

subject: Surgery
question: A 15-year-old girl presented with a slow-growing, rubbery, non-tender swelling on the side of the neck as shown in the image. Examination shows the presence of freckles and skin patches over the face and trunk. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Solt
n to Question 25:
A lateral neck mass in a pediatric patient, which is non-tender and present in association
with freckles, and hyperpigmented skin patches (cafe-au-lait spots) is suggestive of neural tumor
i.e. neurofibroma.
Neurofibroma is a benign tumor arising from the connective tissue of the nerve containing
ectodermal, neural and mesodermal connective tissue components. It can be single or multiple. It
is the most common intradural extramedullary spinal tumor. Neurofibromas can be local,
generalized (von Recklinghausen’s disease) or plexiform types. Patients present with mild pain or
a painless swelling usually in the subcutaneous or cutaneous plane with tingling, numbness and
paraesthesia along the distribution of the nerve. Complications of neurofibroma include cystic
degeneration, erosion into deeper planes, cord compression and paralysis (spinal neurofibroma)
and sarcomatous changes.
Treatment is excision under general or regional anesthesia. Indications for surgery in
neurofibroma include symptomatic patients (pain and pressure symptoms), cosmetic problems
and malignant transformation (5%).
Option A: Cystic hygroma is a type of congenital lymphangioma. This swelling is soft and partially
compressible, which shows positive fluctuation and translucency test.
Option B: Chemodectoma or carotid body tumor is a rare tumor, most commonly present in the
fifth decade and there is an association with pheochromocytoma. ‘These patients usually present
with a slowly enlarging, painless lump at the carotid bifurcation.
4766

subject: Surgery
question: The position of the patient in the image shown below is known as:

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 27:
The above image shows a reverse Trendelenburg position, where the patient's head is elevated 15 -
30 degrees higher than the feet. It is named so, as the position is reverse of the Trendelenburg,
position, where the head end is lower and the feet are at a higher level.
‘The Trendelenburg and reverse Trendelenburg positions are used in surgeries that are required to
move the abdominal viscera, such as the small bowel, away from the operative field. Restraints
and sideboards are placed to prevent the patient from sliding from the bed.
In laparoscopic cholecystectomy, the patient is placed in a reverse ‘Trendelenburg position slightly
rotated to the left. This exposes the fundus of the gallbladder which is retracted towards the
diaphragm.
Other options:
Option A: Lloyd Davies's position is a 30-degree Trendelenburg position with the hips flexed at 15
degrees. It is used for rectal and anal canal surgeries like low anterior resection and
abdominoperineal resection. It can cause compartment syndrome of the calves.
Option B: Jackknife position is a modified prone position where the patient's hips are supported
on a pillow and the table is flexed at a 90-degree angle with the head and feet at a lower level. It is
not used anymore due to the chest compression causing impaired ventilation leading to positional
asphyxia. It was used for hemorrhoids and fissure surgery.
4767

subject: Surgery
question: What will be the most likely associated condition with increased TSH and low T4 levels?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 28:
Increased TSH and low Tq levels are seen in primary hypothyroidism. Hashimoto's thyroiditis is
the only cause of primary hypothyroidism in the given options.
Hashimoto's thyroiditis is an autoimmune disease that results in the destruction of the thyroid
gland and gradual & progressive thyroid failure. It is the most common cause of primary
hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient areas, They also have autoantibodies against thyroid
receptors, thyroid peroxidase and thyroglobulin.
Other options:
4768
Option C: Grave's disease is the most common cause of hyperthyroidism. It is an autoimmune
disorder caused due to autoantibodies against the TSH receptor. There will be elevated T3 and T4,
levels with low TSH levels.
Option B: A TSH-producing pituitary adenoma can have increased TSH levels, but there will be
elevated T3, T4 levels as well.

subject: Surgery
question: In e-FAST which of the following body part is included that is not included in FAST?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 29:
e-FAST is used to additionally assess the presence of free fluid in the thoracic cavity.
FAST stands for focused assessment with sonography for trauma. It is a rapid diagnostic
examination to assess patients with potential thoracoabdominal injuries. It can be performed
within 2-4 minutes.
‘The sequential order in which FAST is performed: pericardial sac, the right upper quadrant
(RUQ), left upper quadrant (LUQ), and pelvis. 4 'Ps' are evaluated in sequence: Pericardial sac >
Perihepatic region > Perisplenic region > Pelvis.
Technique:
+ Following regions are scanned:
+ Subxiphoid: assess for pericardial effusion and left lobe liver injuries
+ Right upper quadrant: assess for right liver injuries, right kidney injury, and Morison pouch
+ Left upper quadrant: assess for splenic injury and left kidney injury
+ Suprapubic region: assess the bladder and pouch of Douglas
An ‘extended FAST or eFAST' scan may be performed: left and right thoracic views to assess for
pneumothorax and hemothorax.

subject: Surgery
question: According to Strasberg’s classification of bile duct injuries, injury to the right aberrant posterior sectoral duct with bile leak is:

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 30:
Injury to the right aberrant posterior sectoral duct associated with bile leak is classified
as Strasberg's type C injury.
Biliary injury may occur post-laparoscopic cholecystectomy. The most common site of bile leak
following cholecystectomy is the cystic duct.
MRCP is the gold standard for complete morphological evaluation of the biliary tree. MRCP with
magnetic resonance angiography is more useful in cases of associated vascular injury. A HIDA
scan can confirm the presence of a bile leak or biliary obstruction.
The Strasberg classification of bile duct injury is widely used to anatomically define bile duct
injuries.
Classification
4769
+ Type C: Bile leak from the divided right posterior sectoral duct.
+ Type D: Bile leak from the main bile duct without major tissue loss.
+ Type E: Injury to the main hepatic duct; classified according to the level of injury
+ Et: Transected main bile duct with a stricture more than 2cm from the hilus.
+ E2: Transected main bile duct with a stricture less than 2cm from the hilus
+ E3: Stricture of the hilus with right and left ducts in communication.
+ E4: Stricture of the hilus with separation of right and left hepatie ducts.
+ Ep: Stricture of the right aberrant sectoral and main bile ducts.
4770

subject: Surgery
question: A bus conductor was diagnosed with varicose veins. On examination, skin eczema and lipodermatosclerosis were noted. According to the CEAP classification system, He would be classified as

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 31:
According to CEAP classification, varicose veins with skin eczema and lipodermatosclerosis come
under the clinical class of C4.
The clinical class of C4 under CEAP includes changes in skin and subcutaneous tissue secondary,
to chronic venous disease. It is further classified as,
+ C4a- pigmentation or eczema
+ C4b- lipodermatosclerosis or atrophie blanche
+ Cge- corona phlebectatiea
Recent 2020 update on the CEAP system:
+ Car for recurrent varicose veins
+ Ce for Corona phlebectatiea
+ C6r for recurrent active venous ulcer.

subject: Surgery
question: Which of the following is the most commonly seen tumor in the retroperitoneal space?

explanation.detail:
Solt
n to Question 32:
Liposarcoma is the most commonly seen tumor in retroperitoneal space.
Retroperitoneal tumors can be benign or malignant. Malignant retroperitoneal tumors may be
mesodermal (75%), neuroectodermal (24%), or embryonic remnant (1%) in origin. Rarely it can
be urologic or metastatic also. The order of frequency of primary retroperitoneal malignancy is as
follows:
+ Liposareoma
+ Leiomyosarcoma
+ Rhabdomyosarcoma
+ Fibrosarcoma
+ Malignant nerve sheath tumors
Liposarcoma is composed of three histologic varieties (listed in order of decreasing frequency)
+ Well-differentiated and undifferentiated liposarcoma
+ Pleomorphic liposarcoma
+ Myxoid/round cell liposareoma
Among the three subtypes, pleomorphic liposarcoma is the most malignant and often
metastasizes, while the well-differentiated is the least malignant. Myxoid liposarcoma has
intermediate malignant behavior.
Diagnosis is confirmed by contrast-enhanced CT with CT of the chest to look for metastases.

Chemotherapeutic agents used are mesna, adriamycin, ifosfamide and dacarbazine (MAID
regimen)

subject: Surgery
question: . . . . . 4741 . . . 2cm is palpated in the right axilla. No mass is palpated in the opposite breast. Which of the following investigations would you perform on this patient?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 33:
The given clinical scenario is suggestive of breast malignancy with locoregional spread with
unlikely distant metastasis. For further evaluation of this lesion, bilateral mammography, a
biopsy of the breast mass and a USG-guided biopsy of the suspicious lymph node need to be
performed in order to determine staging and decide on further management.
Statement 2: PET Scan is usually performed in advanced cases of breast cancer. It is not routinely
used in the assessment of locoregional disease.
Statement 1: Diagnostic mammography is used to evaluate women with abnormal findings such as
a breast mass or nipple discharge. Mammography is more accurate than clinical examination for
the detection of early breast cancers. Mammographic features suggestive of breast cancer include
a solid mass with or without stellate features, microcalcifications, irregular soft tissue shadow,
spiculations and duet distortion.
Statement 3: Pathological diagnosis of carcinoma is made by a core needle biopsy (tru-cut biopsy)
or fine-needle aspiration before any type of treatment is started.
Statement 4: The presence or absence of axillary lymph node involvement in breast cancer is one
of the most important prognostic factors for determining staging and treatment.
Ultrasound-guided lymph node biopsy should be done in patients with suspicious or clinically
palpable lymph nodes.
Ultrasound features suggestive of breast cancer include a hypoechoic, irregular mass with
indistinct, angulated or spiculated margins, acoustic shadowing, ductal extension and a branched
pattern of mass.

subject: Surgery
question: Which of the following is true about the Tillaux sign?
options: Mass is felt in the periumbilical region
Mass is mobile perpendicular to mesentery attachment line

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 35:
Tillaux’'s sign is said to be positive if the mass/swelling is mobile perpendicular to the mesentery
attachment line. Tillaux’s sign is seen in a mesenteric cyst.
Mesenteric cysts occur either in the small intestine (60%) or the colon (40%) and can be classified
as the following:
+ Chylolymphatic (most common type)
+ Enterogenous
+ Urogenital remnant
+ Dermoid
+ Other causes: traumatic hematoma and cyst formation, tuberculous cold abscess of mesentery
and hydatid cyst of the mesentery
They most commonly occur in adults with a mean age of 45 years. It is more common in females
than males (2:1). The most common presentation is a painless abdominal swelling near the
umbilicus, which is smooth and not moving with respiration with Tillaux's triad:
+ Soft, smooth swelling in the umbilical region
+ Freely mobile in a direction perpendicular to the mesentery (Tillauy's sign)
+ Zone of resonance around the cyst
Other presentations include recurrent attacks of abdominal pain due to temporary impaction of a
food bolus in a segment of the bowel narrowed by the cyst and vomiting.
Complications of mesenteric cyst include torsion, rupture, infection and hemorrhage into the
cyst.
Ultrasonography and CT are used to demonstrate mesenteric cysts.
4773
+ Enterogenous cyst is managed by removal of the cyst with resection of the adjacent bowel as it
arises as a diverticulum or duplication from the adjacent bowel
In cases where complete excision is not possible due to the close proximity to vital structures,
partial excision or marsupialization should be performed.
LW iy

subject: Surgery
question: Match the correct answer related to the nerve injuries in various surgeries:

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 36:
A-1, B-2, C-3, and D-4 is the correct option.
During breast surgery, removal of the axillary lymph nodes is commonly done. This carries a
risk of injury to the long thoracic nerve, which originates from the C5-C7 roots of the brachial
plexus. It innervates the serratus anterior muscle. Injury to this nerve during surgery results in
paralysis of the serratus anterior muscle leading to winging of the scapula.
B: The external laryngeal nerve is a motor nerve that supplies the cricothyroid muscle. The close
relationship of the external laryngeal nerve to the superior thyroid artery puts the nerve at
potential risk when the artery is clamped during thyroid surgeries.
C: Excision of the submandibular gland results in potential risk to 3 cranial nerves:
+ Marginal mandibular branch of the facial nerve
+ Lingual nerve
+ Hypoglossal nerve
D: The vagus nerve is at greater risk of injury during surgeries like thymectomy.

subject: Surgery
question: A child 1s diagnosed with congenital hypertrophic pyloric stenosis, which among the following can be seen in this child?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 37:
lert), meavone apnormanues inciuae nypoxahiih, nypocmoremu ana
ELADOLIC UEKALOSIS.
CHPS occurs due to hypertrophy of the circular muscle of the pylorus, which results in
constriction and obstruction of the gastric outlet.
+ Patients commonly present between 2 and 8 weeks of age.
+ First-born male infants are at the highest risk,
+ Infants usually present with projectile, non-bilious vomiting,
+ Clinically, an olive-shaped mass is palpable in the right upper quadrant or epigastrium. When
the infant is given a test feed, visible peristalsis is usually observed moving from left to right (not
from right to left), followed by projectile vomiting.
Metabolic abnormality:
+ Infants with hypertrophic pylorie stenosis develop a characteristic metabolic abnormality i.e.
hypokalemic hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis with paradoxical aciduria
+ Persistent vomiting results in loss of hydrochloric acid, leading to metabolic alkalosis (due to
loss of H+) and hypochloremia (due to loss of C-)
+ Kidneys try to compensate for alkalosis by secreting Na+ and HCO3-. However, sodium loss
stimulates aldosterone secretion through the RAS pathway.
+ Aldosterone causes active reabsorption of sodium in exchange for K+ and H+ ions leading to the
development of hypokalemia and paradoxical aciduria, since the body initially loses H+ ions due
to the loss of HCl, and instead of compensating for that loss, the body is losing more H+ ions
through urine.
Management:
+ The child should be rehydrated and the metabolic abnormality must be corrected before surgery.
+ Ramstedt pyloromyotomy remains the standard surgical procedure for its treatment.

subject: Surgery
question: The components of qSOFA scoring include:

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 38:
‘The quick Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (qSOFA) scoring system does not rely on the
heart rate.
It is a bedside scoring system that may identify patients with suspected infection, who are at
greater risk for sepsis outside the intensive care unit (ICU).
It uses three criteria, assigning one point each:
+ Low blood pressure (SBP100 mmHg)
+ High respiratory rate (222 breaths per min)
+ Altered mental status
The qSOFA suggests potentially life-threatening sepsis when at least two of the parameters are
met.
4775
infection is by determining the sequential (sepsis-related) organ failure assessment (SOFA) score;
which includes an assessment of:
+ Circulation - Systolic BP & need for vasopressors; platelet count
+ Respiration - PaO2/FiO2 & need for mechanical ventilation
+ Mentation - Glasgow Coma Seore
+ Hepatic function - Bilirubin
+ Renal function - Creatinine
Organ dysfunction is identified by a SOFA score of >2
The SOFA score is limited by its requirement of multiple laboratory tests which makes it complex
and time-consuming. In order to simplify the scoring process, the "Sepsis -3" task force
recommended the quick sepsis-related organ dysfunction assessment "qSOFA" score.

subject: Surgery
question: Which of the following is true regarding inguinal hernia?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 39:
‘The medial border of the triangle of doom is bounded by vas deferens and the medial border of
the myopectineal orifice is bounded by the lateral border of the rectus abdominis.
The triangle of doom is bounded
+ Medially by the vas deferens
+ Laterally by the vessels of the spermatic cord (gonadal vessels)
+ Inferiorly by peritoneal reflection
‘The myopectineal orifice of Fruchaud is an osseo-myo-aponeurotie tunnel through which all groin
hernias occur.
It is bounded:
+ Medially by the lateral border of the rectus sheath.
+ Laterally by the iliopsoas muscle.
+ Above by the arched fibers of the internal oblique and transversus abdominis muscle.
+ Below by the pectin pubis and fascia covering it.
4776

subject: Surgery
question: Match the following:

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 40:
A-3, B-1, C-2, and D-4 is the correct option.
A: Uro bags are used for the collection of urine. They are usually attached to Foley's catheter,
which is placed to drain the bladder (commonly in postoperative patients)
B: Nasogastric tube/Ryle's tube is used for nasogastric feeding/stomach wash/aspiration,
C: An underwater seal bag is used for the collection of drainage fluid from the thoracic cavity. It
acts as a one-way valve allowing air to leave the pleural cavity, but it cannot be drawn back in by
the negative pressure that is created in the intrathoracic cavity.
D: A metallic suction cannula is used to clear the operative field by suctioning secretions/blood
and aspirating the fluid from various cysts and in a procedure like a hydrocele.

subject: Surgery
question: A 40-year-old man presents to the ER with severe abdominal pain. On examination, his HR is 110bpm, BP- 88/60mmHg. On per abdomen examination, guarding is present. The erect abdominal X-ray is shown below. Which of the following would you proceed with, for the management of this case?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 41:
The given clinical scenario and erect X-ray abdomen showing gas under the diaphragm are
suggestive of pneumoperitoneum, which is managed by initial resuscitation with IV fluids
followed by exploratory laparotomy.
Pneumoperitoneum is the presence of air or gas in the peritoneal cavity. The most common cause
of pneumoperitoneum is a perforation of a hollow viscus. Other causes of pneumoperitoneum in
adults include peritoneal dialysis, and mechanical ventilation, after procedures like colonoscopy
and endoscopy. In children, it can be caused by necrotizing enterocolitis, hirschsprung's disease,
meconium ileus and mechanical ventilation.
‘The patient presents with abdominal pain and distension, vomiting, fever, constipation,
tachycardia, tachypnea, hypotension, and reduced urine output. Per-abdomen examination can
show guarding and rigidity.
4777
+ Cupola sign is the presence of air underneath the diaphragm, giving a dome-like appearance.
+ Rigler’s sign is where both bowel surfaces are seen together as a sharp margin.
+ Faleiform ligament sign is the outlining of the falciform ligament with free abdominal gas.
+ Liver/gallbladder edge sign is where the sharp margins of the liver/gallbladder are visible due to
the collection of air.
Management includes,
+ Adequate preoperative resuscitation with intravenous fluids followed by correction of electrolyte
imbalance, instituting broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics and
+ Early surgical intervention/laparotomy to address the cause of pneumoperitoneum and prevent
further contamination of the peritoneal cavity.
4778

subject: Surgery
question: Which of the following are indications for surgical intervention in abdominal aortic aneurysms?
options: Size more than 5.5 cm in males
Size more than 5 cm in females

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 1:
All the given are indications for surgical intervention in abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA). The
indications include:
* Size >5.5 cm in males (option A)
+ Size >4.5-5 cm in females (option B)
+ Expansion of >0.5 em within 6 months or >1 cm in one year (option C)
+ Painful, tender aneurysm
+ Thrombosed aneurysm, aneurysm with distal emboli
‘The rupture risk is high for aneurysms >5.5 em and begins to rise exponentially with an
increase in size. Women are at higher risk for rupture than men, with a lower threshold of 4.5 to
5.0 em (depending on the reference used).
Screening is done for:
+ Cardiovascular patients in men and women (60-85 years)
+ Men and women >50 years with positive family history
+ Annual screening in asymptomatic patients with 4-4.5 em size, size >4.5 cm once in every 6
months.
Management of AAA can be done by open repair or endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR). About
75% of infrarenal aneurysms are suitable for endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR), depending on
the morphology of the aneurysm assessed by a CT scan.
Aneurysms that fall below the mentioned indications may safely be followed with CT or
ultrasound at 6-month intervals since long-term outcomes to routine follow-up are equivalent to
earlier surgical repair of the aneurysm. Smoking cessation is very important and reduces the
mortality rate.

subject: Surgery
question: The following image shows the retrograde ureteropyelogram of a patient with loin pain. What is the most probable clinical diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 2:
The given image showing dilated clubbed calyces with stricture of the left ureteropelvic junction
points to a diagnosis of hydronephrosis.
Hydronephrosis refers to the dilatation of the pelvicalyceal system caused by a partial or complete
blockage of urinary flow.
Investigations:
+ Ultrasound is the least invasive technique for diagnosing hydronephrosis.
+ Retrograde pyelography is rarely indicated but will confirm the site of obstruction before
corrective surgery.
+ Intravenous urogram (IVU) is helpful only if the obstructed kidney is functional. In IVU, the
extrarenal pelvis is dilated and the minor calyces lose their normal cupping. In early stages, there
is blunting of calyces followed by its clubbing in the late stages.
+ Arenal isotope scan (DTPA or MAG-3) is the best modality to confirm the obstructive dilatation
of the collecting system.

Solution to Question 3:
Ammonium urate stones are most likely to be seen in patients with laxative abuse.
Loss of potassium and sodium in laxative abuse causes intracellular acidosis, which increases the
production of ammonia in urine. Dehydration and sodium loss further exacerbate hypokalemia
and reduces the urinary volume, resulting in hyperuricemia. Highly concentrated uric acid and
ammonia promote the formation of ammonium urate stones.
4789
‘The below image shows an ammonium urate stone.

subject: Surgery
question: A patient is confused, opens his eyes on painful stimuli, flexes one limb on painful stimuli, and can localize the stimulus with the other limb. His Glasgow Coma Score is

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 4:
The GCS in this patient is 11.
+ The score for verbal response: 4, because he is confused.
+ The score for eye-opening: 2, because his eyes are opening to only painful stimuli.
+ The score for best motor response: 5, because he can localize pain.
Therefore, the GCS score is (4#2+5) = 11.

subject: Surgery
question: A patient presents with a painless swelling of the neck. The histology of the tissue is as given in the image below. What is the diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 5:
‘The slide shows the histopathology of Hashimoto thyroiditis, with:
+ Follicular destruction
+ Reduced colloid with increased interstitial connective tissue
+ Small lymphocyte and plasma cell infiltration
+ A well-developed germinal centre may be seen
+ Hurthle (Askanazy) cells that have abundant eosinophilic granular cytoplasm lining the follicles
Hashimoto's thyroiditis is the most common cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient areas. It
is also known as struma lymphomatosa or lymphocytic thyroiditis. It is an autoimmune disease
that results in the destruction of the thyroid gland and gradual & progressive thyroid failure.
Clinical features:
+ Most prevalent between 45-65 years of age
+ More common in females
+ Painless enlargement of the thyroid
+ Hashitoxicosis: When the auto-antibodies attack and damage the follicles, there is a sudden
release of thyroid hormones from these follicles. This leads to a transient phase of thyrotoxicosis.
+ Hypothyroidism: Since the follicles are damaged and cannot form the hormones anymore, these
patients tend to be in a state of hypothyroidism.
Diagnosis:
+ T3 and 74 are low
+ TSH is raised
+ Autoantibodies against:
+ Thyroid receptors
+ Thyroid peroxidase (TPO)
+ Thyroglobulin
‘These patients are managed with thyroid hormone replacement therapy.
In patients with long-standing goiter, there is a risk of compressive features and the development
of thyroid lymphoma. Such cases can be treated with total thyroidectomy. It may also predispose
to papillary thyroid carcinoma.
Other options:
4791

subject: Surgery
question: A 45-year-old male patient who presented with a scrotal swelling underwent an orchidectomy. The image below shows the histopathology findings. What is your diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 6:
Based on the gross and microscopic appearance of the specimen, the diagnosis is seminoma.
‘The gross specimen shows a well-circumscribed, pale, gray-white, fleshy, homogenous mass, with
a lobulated cut surface, usually devoid of hemorrhage or necrosis. Histopathology shows uniform
tumor cells with abundant clear cytoplasm, distinct cell borders, and large central nuclei. There is
a sparse lymphocytic infiltrate. Classically, they have a fried egg appearance.
Other options:
Option B: Yolk-sac tumor has a yellowish-white, mucinous appearance. Histologically, they are
typically composed of a lace-like (reticular) network of medium-sized cuboidal, flattened, or
spindled cells, which generally show less cytologic atypia than embryonal carcinoma. In
approximately 50% of tumors, structures resembling endodermal sinuses (Schiller-Duval bodies)
may be seen; these consist of a mesodermal core with a central capillary and a visceral and
parietal layer of cells resembling primitive glomeruli.
Option C: Embryonal cell carcinoma appears variegated with foci of hemorrhage or necrosis.
Histologically, the cells grow in alveolar or tubular patterns, sometimes with papillary folds. More
undifferentiated lesions may display sheets of cells with cleft-like spaces. The neoplastic cells have
an epithelial appearance, are large and anaplastic, and have hyperchromatic nuclei with
prominent nucleoli. The cell borders are usually indistinct, and there is considerable variation in
cell and nuclear size and shape (pleomorphism). Mitotic figures and tumor giant cells are
frequently seen.
Option D: Teratomas also have a variegated appearance but have cysts reflecting the multiplicity
of tissue types. Histologically, collections of differentiated cells or organoid structures originating
in all 3 germ layers can be seen. Elements may be mature (resembling various adult tissues) or
immature (sharing histologic features with fetal or embryonic tissue).

subject: Surgery
question: A surgeon sees a necrotic bowel when operating on a child for an inguinal hernia. What is true about the resection and anastomosis of the bowel?
options: The surgeon must use chromic catgut
The surgeon must not include the submucosa in the sutures
The surgeon must use the single-layer, extramucosal suture technique
The surgeon must use the single-layer seromuscular suture technique

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 7:
The standard technique used currently for resection anastomosis of the bowel is a one-layer extra
mucosal suture technique since it causes the least tissue necrosis and luminal narrowing.
Interrupted and continuous single-layer suture techniques are adequate and safe,
‘The extra mucosal suture must also include the submucosa because this layer has a high collagen
content and it is the most stable suture layer in all sections of the gastrointestinal tract.
4794

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 8:
‘The given clinical scenario of a woman with reddish firm nodule on her chest and histology
showing multiple dilated vascular structures with a single layer of epithelial cells is suggestive of a
hemangioma.
Hemangioma is a benign tumor caused by the abnormal growth of blood vessels. Types of
hemangioma are capillary hemangioma (most common), juvenile/ strawberry hemangioma,
cavernous hemangioma and pyogenic hemangioma.
Indications for immediate treatment are complications such as ulceration or those pertaining to
the location of the mass (e.g. visual issues due to a large hemangioma over the eyelid).
‘Treatment options include:
+ Beta-blockers - oral propranolol
+ Steroids - intralesional triamcinolone, topical clobetasol, systemic steroids
+ Interferon alfa-2a and vincristine - for steroid-resistant lesions
+ Local resection with cutting cautery or carbon dioxide laser
‘The given image shows a flat to elevated, irregular, pinkish-red mass over the skin of the abdomen
suggestive of a strawberry hemangioma.

subject: Surgery
question: Which is the most common organism causing acute bacterial prostatitis?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 9:
‘The most common organism causing acute bacterial prostatitis (ABP) is Escherichia coli
It is commonly caused by an ascending urethral infection or reflux of infected urine into prostatic
duets, Sexually transmissible infections and other urinary pathogens, such as Staphylococcus
aureus and Streptococcus faecalis may also be responsible
The patient presents with fever, perineal pain, chills and rigor, and dysuria. Per rectal
examination will reveal a tender, boggy prostate. Care should be taken to avoid aggressive
palpation or prostatic massage as it can lead to bacterial dissemination and sepsis.
Urine examination and culture should be done. Urine examination reveals threads in urine.
‘Management:
Patients with systemic signs of infection should be admitted for IV antibiotics, hydration, and
monitoring of laboratory parameters.
Patients who have no signs of systemic infection, who can tolerate oral intake, and who have no
urinary retention can be treated with oral antibiotics. Antibiotics are given for 2-6 weeks to ensure
the eradication of the infection as antibiotics don't penetrate the prostate capsule easily.
For outpatient therapy: Ciprofloxacin 500 mg oral every 12 hours or levofloxacin 500 mg OD or
trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole double-strength tablet oral every 12 hours can be used.
For hospitalized patients: Intravenous ciprofloxacin or levofloxacin with or without an
aminoglycoside (gentamicin or tobramycin) or intravenous beta-lactam with or without an
aminoglycoside. Gram-positive infections are treated with cephalosporins (cephalexin, cefazolin)
or dicloxacillin and vancomycin.

subject: Surgery
question: Which is the best graft for a femoropopliteal bypass?

explanation.detail:
Solt
n to Question 11:
The great saphenous vein (GSV) is the best as it lasts longer than polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE),
dacron, and human umbilical vein as a graft in a femoropopliteal bypass.
‘The GSV is the conduit of choice for arterial bypass grafts below the inguinal ligament.
For bypass above the inguinal ligament, while both GSV and PTFE grafts have similar patency
rates in the first 2 years, the GSV is superior thereafter and hence preferred over PTFE or any
other grafts.
The ipsilateral GSV is the conduit of choice for femoropopliteal bypass. When it is not available,
contralateral GSV should be used. Other veins are preferred when both ipsilateral and
contralateral GSV is not available and they include a small saphenous vein, cephalic vein, and
basilic vein.
Non-autogenous grafts are used for patients who lack a suitable venous conduit. The most
commonly used prosthetic conduit is PTFE. Recent studies have shown that heparin-bonded
PTFE is superior to compared to PTFE without heparin,

subject: Surgery
question: A probe is inserted into the duct from which the nipple discharge is emerging. A tennis racquet incision is made and the flap is dissected to reach the duct. The duct is then excised. This procedure is known as?
options: Wide excision of the lactiferous duct.

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 13:
Microdochectomy is the excision of the entire lactiferous duct (single duct disease). It is done for
duct papilloma.
Microdochectomy:
+ Ina duct papilloma where no palpable mass is present, the duct is expressed to observe the
discharge.
+ A fine probe or stiff nylon suture is gently inserted into the duct, and the probe is secured to the
nipple skin using silk sutures to prevent displacement during the procedure.
+ A tennis racquet shaped incision is made to elevate the skin flaps, and the duct with the probe is
excised along with the skin flap for 5 cm until the papilloma is identified (The papilloma will
always be located within 5 cm from the duet orifice).
+ The wound is closed without a drain, and a compression bandage is applied.
It is ideal to rule out DCIS by frozen section biopsy.
Cone excision and wide excision are done under major duct excision (Adair-Haldfield operation).
Itis done for duct ectasias with multiple duct involvement and multiple papillomas.

subject: Surgery
question: Which of the following is true about chromic catgut?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 14:
Chromic catgut is absorbed through macrophage phagocytosis and enzymatic degradation within
90 days.
It is made from collagen derived from healthy sheep or cattle. It is then tanned with chromium
salts to improve handling and resist degradation in tissue.
4798
Itis used to ligate superficial vessels, suture subcutaneous tissues, and is used for stomas and
other tissues that heal rapidly. It is not used in tissues that heal slowly and require prolonged
support.

Morris retractor can be single or double-bladed. It is used to retract abdominal wall incisions, loin

It can be used to improve visibility on one side of an incision. Thus, it is valuable during the initial

be kept underneath prior to placing this retractor. It is used for major abdominal surgeries,
bladder surgeries and lumbar sympathectomy.

subject: Surgery
question: Extra mammary Paget's disease is commonly seen in the:

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 16:
Extramammary Paget's disease arises most frequently on the vulva. Other sites include the
perineum, scrotum, perianal skin, and penis.
Extramammary Paget's disease (EMPD) can be primary or secondary.
+ Primary EMPD is more common and occurs in the absence of an underlying malignancy,
thought to arise from intraepidermal parts of apocrine glands or from pluripotent cells from the
epidermis.
+ Secondary EMPD arises from an underlying adnexal carcinoma or internal malignancy due to
the epidermotropic spread of malignant cells from the underlying tumor.
Visceral malignancies associated with EMPD are carcinomas of the colon, rectum, bladder,
urethra, cervix, and prostate. The patient presents with well-defined, moist, erythematous scaly
plaques and severe pruritis. Lichenification and excoriations may also be seen.
Diagnosis is confirmed by punch, wedge, or excisional skin biopsy. A systemic workup should also
be done to identify any underlying malignancy and they include colonoscopy, cystoscopy,
colposcopy, and pelvic examination with pap smear in females.
Treatment:
+ Mohs microscopic surgery with 5 em margin clearance
+ Radiotherapy
+ Topical 5-fluorouracil and imiquimod
+ Chemotherapy
+ Photodynamic therapy
The given image below shows extramammary Paget's disease of the vulva.

options: SAP AA CAMIOSVEE RL A AN AY eee BY eee ee eee eee ee
I I d) Percutaneous transperitoneal biopsy

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 17:
Transgastric or transduodenal FNAC or trucut biopsy performed under endoscopic ultrasound
(EUS) guidance is the best method to obtain tissue for the diagnosis of pancreatic carcinoma. This
is because it avoids the spillage of tumor cells into the peritoneal cavity.
Endoscopic ultrasonography with a fine needle aspiration (EUS-FNA) is done if CT has failed to
demonstrate a tumor. When a tissue diagnosis is required prior to surgery to distinguish between
inflammation and neoplasia, EUS-FNA is helpful.
Diagnostic laparoscopy prior to resection can spare an unnecessary laparotomy by identifying
small peritoneal and liver metastases.
Percutaneous transperitoneal biopsy of potentially resectable pancreatic tumors is avoided.

subject: Surgery
question: An abdominal mass in a patient with congenital hypertrophic pyloric stenosis is best seen
options: During palpation over the left hypochondrium

explanation.detail:
Solt
n to Question 18:
Ina child with congenital hypertrophic pyloric stenosis (CHPS), the abdominal mass is best seen
during test feed in the right upper quadrant.
Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis most presents with non-bilious projectile vomiting. It is
common between 2 and 8 weeks of age. Hypertrophy of the circular muscle of the pylorus results
in constriction and obstruction of the gastric outlet. Male infants are at a higher risk.
Clinically, when the infant is given a test feed, an olive-shaped mass is palpable in the right upper
quadrant and visible peristalsis is usually observed moving from left to right across the abdomen.
Infants with hypertrophic pyloric stenosis develop a characteristic metabolic
abnormality- hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis. Persistent vomiting results in
loss of hydrochloric acid leading to the development of metabolic alkalosis (due to loss of H+)
and hypochloremia (due to loss of Cl).
4802
Management: Rehydration and metabolic correction prior to definitive surgery - Ramstedt's
pyloromyotomy.

The incision for a fasciotomy involves the skin, subcutaneous tissue, superficial fascia, and deep
fascia.
Fasciotomy involves incising the deep muscle fascia and is best carried out via longitudinal
incisions of skin, subcutaneous fat, and superficial and deep fascia. The muscle will be seen
bulging out through the fasciotomy opening.
‘The lower limb can be decompressed via two incisions, each being lateral to the subcutaneous
border of the tibia. This gives access to the two posterior compartments and the leg's peroneal and
anterior compartments.

subject: Surgery
question: Which of the following statements is true about Crohn's disease?
options: For effective treatment of a Crohn&#x27;s mass, only antibiotics are prescribed.
Symptomatic relapse after surgery is not related to the presence of disease during resection.
The proximal ileum is most commonly involved in Crohn&#x27;s disease.
On endoscopic examination, continuous lesions are visualised, which are diagnostic of Crohn&#x27;s disease.

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 20:
Symptomatic relapse of Crohn's disease after surgery is not related to the presence of the disease
during resection.
The course of the disease after surgery is unpredictable since surgery does not cure Crohn's
disease. Instead, the probability of relapse increases with the time since resection.
Other options:
Option A: Antibiotics alone cannot treat a Crohn's mass effectively. Abscesses can be treated with
metronidazole and ciprofloxacin and should also undergo drainage (and resection if needed).
4803
irregular areas of inflamed mucosa that are ulcerated with mucopurulent exudate separated by

subject: Surgery
question: Question text was not included in the source. Pick the correct option below.

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 21:
Aphthous ulcers, surrounded by a rim of erythematous mucosa, are the earliest lesions seen in a
patient with Crohn's disease during endoscopy.
Crohn's colitis usually spares the rectum and is most severe in the ileocaecal region.
+ In early Crohn's disease, small, punched-out aphthous ulcers are often seen (the result of
submucosal lymphoid follicle expansion).
+ In moderate disease, the aphthous uleers coalesce into larger ulcers, which may take on the
appearance of a star (stellate ulcers)
+ As disease severity increases, submucosal edema and injury can result in cobblestoning of the
mucosa (option A) and eventually the formation of serpiginous uleers, which are typical findings
in Crohn's disease.
Thorn-like extraluminal projections in barium studies are known as rose-thorn appearance, and
are a typical sign of Crohn's disease. They represent deep penetrating linear ulcers or fissurings
typically seen within stenosed terminal ileum with a thickened wall. (Options C and D)

question: The Van Nuys grading system for ductal carcinoma in situ does not include:

explanation.detail:
Solt
n to Question 22:
Staining for estrogen and progesterone receptors is not part of the Van Nuys system used for
grading DCIS.
The components of the Van Nuys prognostic index (VNPI) are as follows:
+ Size of the tumor
+ Extent of resection margin
+ Histological variant of DCIS - based on nuclear grade, presence of necrosis, presence of
microcalcification
+ Age of the patient
Patients with a high score in this grading system benefit from radiotherapy after excision, whereas
those with low-grade and completely excised tumors, need no further treatment.
‘A score ranging from 1 (lesions with the best prognosis) to 3 (lesions with the worst prognosis) is
given for each of the four prognostic predictors. Total scores range from 4 (least likely to recur) to
12 (most likely to recur).
Note: The type of microcalcification seen on imaging, helps in determining the nuclear grade of
DCIS. While the linear branching type of microcalcification is associated with high nuclear grade,
fine granular type is associated with low nuclear grade.

subject: Surgery
question: Which of the following statements are true with regards to a keloid scar?
options: The extent does not cross the wound margins
Wide excision of the keloid scar prevents recurrence
Histopathology of a keloid scar shows reduced collagen and increased vascularity
A keloid scar forms due to increase in the level of growth factors

explanation.detail:
Solt
n to Question 23:
Keloid scars are associated with elevated levels of growth factor leading to increased proliferation
of fibrous tissue.
A keloid scar is defined as excessive scar tissue that extends beyond the boundaries of the original
incision or wound (option A) and rarely regresses on its own. It is more common in
darker-pigmented ethnicities.
It can arise from surgery, burns, skin inflammation, infections, ear piercing, and even
spontaneously. It occurs 3 months to years after the initial insult with even minor injuries causing
keloid formation.
It most commonly occurs on the skin of the earlobe, deltoid, presternal, upper back, and rarely
extends into the subcutaneous tissue.
‘The histology of keloid scars shows excess collagen and hypervascularity with collagen fibres
arranged haphazardly in loose sheets (option C).
‘Treatment: Keloid scars do not improve spontaneously with time and excision alone has a high
rate of recurrence (option B). The following can be done:
+ Pressure - local moulds or elastic garments.
+ Silicone gel sheeting,
+ Intralesional triamcinolone injection.
+ Excision and steroid injections.
+ Excision and postoperative radiation (external beam or brachytherapy).
+ Intralesional excision.
+ Laser to reduce redness.
+ Vitamin E and palm oil massage.

subject: Surgery
question: Which of the following is not true about Barrett's esophagus?

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 25:
Mucus-secreting goblet cells (not foveolar cells) are characteristic of Barrett's esophagus, as there
is intestinal metaplasia of the esophagus. Foveolar cells are the mucus-secreting cells in the
stomach.
Barrett's esophagus is a complication of chronic gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), and it
is estimated to occur in about 10% of individuals with symptomatic GERD. Symptoms include
heartburn, regurgitation and sometimes dysphagia. Obese white men over the age of 60
years represent the highest-risk group.
Intestinal metaplasia in the esophagus occurs due to chronic irritation of the esophagus in GERD
(option A) since intestinal epithelium is better suited to handling gastric acid exposure. Hence,
miucus-secreting goblet cells are characteristic of Barrett's esophagus. Since all metaplasia are
reversible, Barrett's esophagus is also reversible (option D). Only 0.2 to 1% of Barrett's esophagus
leads to dysplasia and adenocarcinoma.
Alician blue is used in the staining of the biopsy specimen, as it stains the goblet cells (option C).
Mucicarmine is the specific stain.
Seattle protocol is used for the management of Barrett's esophagus.
Management of Barrett's Esophagus
(Seattle Protoco),

The shock index is a hemodynamic stability indicator. In patients with shock, it is a

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 1:
‘The given image shows the suture material exiting the knot is on the same side as the entry i.e no
cross-over is seen. This is seen in a reef knot.
A knot is a fastening made of threads intertwined together or on themselves. Each knot consists
of- 2 half hitches placed in sequential throws. Squaring refers to tightening the knot/half-hitch in a
manner that the threads lay flat due to the alternating direction of half-hitches in adjacent layers.
4817
half-hitch
+ Reef knot: half-hitch followed by a second half-hitch after squaring.
+ Triple throw knot: Reef knot with another half-hitch knot for added security. Knots made with
prolene sutures require 6-8 throws to prevent slippage.
+ Surgeon's knot: Modification of the reef knot with two half-hitches in the first layer.
+ Slip knot: Modification of either granny/reef knot formed when one end of the thread is kept taut
while tightening the knot.

subject: Surgery
question: Which of the following drugs causes carcinoma bladder?

explanation.detail:
Solt
n to Question 2:
Cyclophosphamide (an alkylating agent) causes bladder cancer
The risk of bladder cancer with cyclophosphamide is directly proportional to the duration and
intensity of cyclophosphamide treatment. Phosphoramide mustard is the primary mutagenic
metabolite that causes bladder cancer in patients exposed to cyclophosphamide.
Painless, gross hematuria is the most common symptom that is indicative of a bladder carcinoma
until proven otherwise.
Depending upon the depth of invasion, bladder carcinoma can be divided into
+ Flat, non-invasive carcinoma in situ (CIS)
+ Non-muscle invasive bladder cancer (70% of cases) - does not invade detrusor muscle but can
invade lamina propria.
+ Muscle-invasive bladder cancer - invades the detrusor muscle.

question: Dohlman’s procedure is for:

explanation.detail:
Solution to Question 4:
Dohiman’s procedure is done for Zenker's diverticulum.
It is a transoral endoscopic approach, that ablates the septum between the diverticulum and the
cervical esophagus. It can be used for diverticula between 2 and 5 cm in size
Other options:
Option A: Meckel's diverticulum is a remnant of the vitellointestinal duct and is treated by

Option C: Bochadlek’s hernia is a congenital posterolateral defect on the left side of the diaphragm
